input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper level chassis layers and autonomy elements. It operates within the Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) domain for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), where resource-constrained Electronic Control Units (ECUs) necessitate computation offloading and low-latency communication for safety-critical operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring via a user interface (UI) that displays real-time vehicle state data, projected driving paths, and detected object tracks, enabling data viewing, saving, and report generation; (2) Computation Offloading where the ACC system (lead vehicle detection, speed calculation based on distance) is offloaded from on-board ECUs to cloud-based resources, with results sent back for actuation; and (3) Remote Management allowing operators to enable/disable ACC, set desired speeds, and issue emergency braking commands remotely. These services target operators for monitoring and management, and developers for troubleshooting, with the system categorized as a Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human-in-the-Loop per David and Bork's taxonomy.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation utilizes Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) for middleware communication, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for hardware acceleration, and application modules developed in Python 3.8 and C++ for low-latency data processing and control logic.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for coordination between devices to enable computation offloading in safety-critical vehicular applications, constrained by hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system employs an entity-based architecture with three distinct entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) deployed on separate hardware components. Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package containing one or more ROS2 nodes. The User Interface FE is split into two subcomponents (3D Visualization and Control Panel), while the Physical Twin Entity, a deviation from ISO 23247, handles general automotive functionality such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA) with modifications, mapping software components to Functional Entities (FEs) implemented as ROS2 packages. The system is structured into three entities: Physical Twin Entity (hosting resource-specific and general automotive functionality), Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), and User Entity (3D Visualization and Control Panel). Each entity is deployed on separate hardware, with the Physical Twin Entity representing a significant deviation from the standard's OME.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models include Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking using DBSCAN for clustering followed by Kalman filter for object tracking, and Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) for speed computation. Data sources comprise sensor inputs from LiDAR and depth cameras, ego vehicle state data (speed, steering angle), and detected objects. Data management involves SQL database storage for persistent data, serialization via Data Translation Functional Entities for protocol conversion, and unit conversions to ensure consistency. The Digital Twin Entity represents the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), with Functional Entities handling specific tasks such as data processing and actuation control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller for motor control and a Steering Servo for steering adjustment. The Actuation Functional Entity converts desired speed into motor voltage commands as per Requirement R6 for Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) response. Control mechanisms are managed through the Data Collection and Device Control Entity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, collecting ego vehicle speed, steering angle, and detected objects data as per Requirement R2. No additional sensor specifications such as sampling rates or accuracy metrics are detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requirements emphasize real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections as stated in the Introduction. Validation includes Requirement R7 enforcing network latency of at most 100 ms and Requirement R8 ensuring clock synchronization accuracy within 100 ms. These constraints ensure system responsiveness and temporal consistency for accurate Digital Twin operations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw LiDAR point cloud data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component to generate object tracks. Vehicle state information and object tracks are collected by the Data Collecting FE. The Data Translation FE serializes all data streams, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions. Internal software components communicate via ROS2, while inter-entity data transfer occurs over Wi-Fi. Preprocessing steps include clustering and tracking of point cloud data to produce structured object tracks.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands from the ACC Controlling FE and Control Panel User Interface FE are serialized by the Data Translation component and transmitted over Wi-Fi using UDP to the Actuation FE for motor and steering servo actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Internal communication between software components uses ROS2, while inter-entity communication occurs over Wi-Fi with UDP transport. The system adheres to Requirement R7, maintaining network latency of at most 100 ms. The architecture employs a distributed network topology with ROS2 for intra-entity and Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication, ensuring reliable data exchange within specified latency constraints.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Each entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity) is deployed on dedicated hardware components. Specific hardware includes NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano for edge processing of sensor data. Compute resources are allocated to cloud or edge based on suitability, as per Section III.A. The deployment model supports both edge and cloud environments, with NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano handling real-time tasks and cloud resources providing scalability for data storage and processing.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process involves DTS Requirements, Architecture, and Implementation phases, with validation through demonstration and testing as outlined in Section IV.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Functional Entities (FEs) transform raw data into human-interpretable formats, and the User Entity provides interfaces for monitoring data and controlling Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs), enabling real-time decision support through visualized insights and operational commands.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247:2021 standard, which includes four parts: Part 1 (Overview and general principles), Part 2 (Reference architecture), Part 3 (Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements), and Part 4 (Information exchange). Part 2 defines an entity-based reference architecture comprising Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs) as physical entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products), Data Collection and Device Control Entity for data collection and control, Digital Twin Entity for digital representation, and User Entity for user interaction interfaces. Each entity is composed of Functional Entities (FEs) handling tasks such as actuation, data transformation, and user interfacing.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description is highly specific and accurately captures the physical twin's scale, retrofitting nature, hardware architecture, and the specific ADAS/ACC domain with its constraints (ECU offloading, latency). Supported by quotes: 'physical twin (PT) is a 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower and upper level chassis, and the autonomy elements', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction is comprehensive, correctly identifying the three specific services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management), their detailed functionalities (UI display, ACC logic, remote commands), target users (operators/developers), and the specific taxonomy classification. Supported by quotes: 'Data Monitoring... allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading... offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop'.

The description correctly identifies key technologies: 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2)' as middleware, 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0' as the SDK, and the programming languages 'Python 3.8' and 'C++'. However, it omits 'MATLAB/Simulink' which is explicitly mentioned in the source text as being used for 'more complex systems'. Therefore, while correct, it is missing a technical detail present in the source.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately summarizes the time synchronization and latency requirements found in the document. Specifically, it correctly cites the 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy required for coordination and computation offloading, noting the constraint of hardware limitations. This is supported by requirement R8: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms... We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations' and the context of R7 regarding computation offloading.

The description correctly identifies the three entities (Physical, Digital, User) and the specific implementation details regarding ROS2 packages and the split of the User Interface FE. It accurately highlights the deviation regarding the Physical Twin Entity hosting general automotive functionality like Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking. Supported by text: 'Each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component' and 'The existence of this entity marks a departure from the ISO 23247 RA... host general automotive functionality, such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking'.

The extraction provides a solid overview of the architecture, its adherence to ISO 23247, and the mapping of FEs to ROS2 packages. It correctly describes the three entities and the deviation regarding the Physical Twin Entity. However, it misses some specific details regarding the coordination mechanisms, such as the explicit role of the 'Data Translation' FE for inter-entity communication and the specific network (Wi-Fi) used, which limits its depth slightly.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. A review of the source text confirms that there is no mention of integration with external systems, interoperability with other Digital Twins, or enterprise systems. The text focuses exclusively on the internal architecture of the specific ACC Digital Twin system. Therefore, the statement is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

However, the statement that control mechanisms are managed 'through the Data Collection and Device Control Entity' is slightly inconsistent with the specific case study architecture in Section IV-B, which states 'actuation... are organized into the Physical Twin Entity', though the generic standard definition in II-A supports the Data Collection entity's role in controlling the OME.

This extraction is precise and correctly identifies the sensors ('Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera') and the data collected per Requirement R2 ('ego vehicle’s speed', 'steering angle', 'detected objects'). The explicit statement that 'No additional sensor specifications... are detailed in the document' is accurate based on the provided text, avoiding hallucination.

The extraction is highly accurate and well-supported. It cites the Introduction for the definition of DTs as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections'. It correctly references 'Requirement R7' regarding 'network latency of at most 100 ms' and 'Requirement R8' regarding 'clock synchronization accuracy within 100 ms' from Section IV-A. The reasoning connecting these constraints to system responsiveness and temporal consistency aligns with the text's explanation of these requirements.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Accurately describes the three phases of the engineering process detailed in Section IV: 'definition of DTS requirements', 'architectural design', and 'implementation of DTS services'.
